summary: As shipped on linear.app (2026-07-03), there are no numeric color ramps. The palette is a small set of named primitives in :root (--color-blue: #4ea7fc, --color-red: #eb5757, --color-green: #27a644, --color-orange, --color-yellow, --color-indigo, --color-teal, --color-white, --color-black, plus campaign colors like --color-linear-plan) and per-theme semantic tokens (backgrounds, text, borders, accent). Translucency is expressed as individual tokens with 8-digit hex values (--color-bg-translucent: #ffffff0d), not as alpha ramp variants.

evidence.note: Content-hashed bundle referenced from the linear.app homepage on the retrieval date.
Page inspected: linear.app homepage (marketing site). The logged-in app is behind authentication and was not inspected.

notes: Scope: marketing-site CSS. The product app may carry a different or larger token set; the first-party blog implies themes are generated in app code.

answer.algorithm: LCH-based theme generation from three theme variables (base color, accent color, contrast)

summary: Linear's team documents that themes are generated, not hand-authored per theme: 'instead of having to define 98 specific variables for each theme, we defined three: base color, accent color, and contrast', working in LCH because it is 'perceptually uniform' (migrated from HSL). The 2020 custom-themes changelog corroborates the derivation approach: 'You only need to set a few colors ... which we then use to generate complimentary shades for borders and elevated boxes.' The generation code is closed; shipped marketing CSS carries only its static output plus runtime color-mix(in lch, ...) derivations.

evidence.excerpt: LCH has the benefit that it's perpetually uniform, meaning a red and a yellow color with lightness 50 will appear roughly equally light to the human eye. [...] instead of having to define 98 specific variables for each theme, we defined three: base color, accent color, and contrast.
You only need to set a few colors, such as the background, text and accent colors, which we then use to generate complimentary shades for borders and elevated boxes.

summary: Working space is LCH per the first-party redesign post; the color math lives in application code, and shipped CSS contains no lch() literals — theme blocks are pre-resolved sRGB hex (including 8-digit hex alpha). LCH surfaces at runtime only in derived tokens: --color-selection-bg: color-mix(in lch, var(--color-brand-bg), black 10%). Wide gamut is marginal as shipped: a single primitive is upgraded — @supports (color:color(display-p3 0 0 0)) { :root { --color-blue: color(display-p3 .431 .6816 .9988) } }.

evidence.excerpt: LCH has the benefit that it's perpetually uniform [...]
evidence.note: Corroborates LCH as the working space for theme generation.

summary: Contrast is a first-class input to theme generation — 'we defined three: base color, accent color, and contrast', which 'allows us to automatically include super high-contrast themes for users who need it for accessibility reasons' (first-party blog). But no contrast standard (WCAG 2.x or APCA), no numeric targets, and no per-token guarantees are published, and none are observable in shipped CSS. Whether the generator enforces measurable ratios or the contrast variable is a perceptual dial is unknown.

evidence.excerpt: This allows us to automatically include super high-contrast themes for users who need it for accessibility reasons.

evidence.note: Static theme blocks carry no contrast metadata; no ratio-related comments or structures survive in shipped CSS.

notes: Answered at the mechanism level; enforcement/standard fields are honestly 'unknown' — closed generation code, and CSS inspection cannot reveal generator-internal targets. Flagged for maintainer per plan STOP condition.

summary: As shipped on linear.app (2026-07-03): a thin primitive layer of named colors, then two parallel semantic generations inside each [data-theme] block — an ordinal-ranked set (bg/text/border × primary…quinary) and a newer surface-oriented set (bg-level-0..3, fg-*, line-*, accent-*) covering the same ground — plus a handful of component-scoped tokens (button-invert, header). The coexistence of both semantic sets suggests a migration in progress.

Beyond color, shipped tokens cover typography in depth (font sizes micro→title9, weights 400/510/590/680, composed shorthands like --title-4), motion (--speed-* durations plus a full easing library --ease-in/out/in-out-{quad..circ}), elevation (--shadow-tiny/low/medium/high/stack-low plus bg-level surfaces), cursors (--cursor-pointer, --cursor-disabled, --cursor-tooltip), z-layers (--layer-dialog), scrollbar and focus-ring primitives. No spacing scale (only layout constants like --header-height, --page-padding-inline), no radius scale (only --rounded-full: 9999px), no breakpoint or opacity tokens.

answer.caseStyle: kebab-case (camelCase leaks in motion tokens, e.g. --speed-regularTransition)
answer.pattern: --color-{category}-{rank|role}: ordinal ranks (primary, secondary, tertiary, quaternary, quinary), numeric elevation levels (bg-level-0..3), and role modifiers (translucent, tint, hover, strong)

summary: Kebab-case --color-{category}-{rank} grammar with ordinal ranks rather than numeric steps; elevation gets numeric levels (bg-level-0..3). Themes apply via a data-theme attribute on <html> (dark/light/glass), each theme a full custom-property override block; light-dark resolution additionally uses lightningcss polyfill variables (--lightningcss-light/--lightningcss-dark) and per-theme color-scheme declarations. An inline script persists the choice under the 'website-theme' localStorage key.

evidence.note: Theme attribute and the theme-bootstrap script arguments in the homepage HTML.

summary: Three themes ship on linear.app (2026-07-03): dark, light, and glass (a translucent dark variant with white-alpha surfaces, e.g. --color-bg-secondary: #ffffff08), selected by data-theme on <html>. All themes re-point the same semantic token names — classic token-repointing over a stable vocabulary, with color-scheme set per theme. In the product, high-contrast themes are generated from the contrast theme variable per the first-party blog. Whether the marketing site follows prefers-color-scheme by default could not be determined from static assets (the choice persists in localStorage).

evidence.excerpt: This allows us to automatically include super high-contrast themes for users who need it for accessibility reasons.

answer.colorSource: --focus-ring-color: var(--color-indigo) in :root; overridden to #0006 in the light theme; component rules also use var(--color-accent)

summary: Focus is tokenized as a first-class group in :root — --focus-ring-color: var(--color-indigo); --focus-ring-width: 2px; --focus-ring-offset: 2px; --focus-ring-outline: var(--focus-ring-width) solid var(--focus-ring-color) — with the light theme swapping the color to a translucent black (#0006). Shipped component rules consistently gate on :focus-visible, typically 'outline: 2px solid var(--color-accent); outline-offset: 2px'. Notably the composed --focus-ring-outline token and the accent-based component rules coexist — two focus color sources in one codebase.

evidence.note: Same pattern recurs across multiple shipped component bundles.

summary: The shipped Button bundle consumes semantic tokens almost exclusively — 16 distinct color variables led by --color-text-primary (8 uses), --color-bg-tertiary (5) and --color-bg-primary (4), plus dedicated component tokens (--color-button-invert-bg, --color-button-invert-bg-hover); it never references a raw primitive like --color-blue directly. A handful of translucent literals are hardcoded for sheen/border effects (#ffffff08, #ffffff26, #00000014). Variant names cannot be recovered from minified class-hash CSS, so the variant list is left empty rather than guessed.

evidence.excerpt: var(--color-text-primary) ×8, var(--color-bg-tertiary) ×5, var(--color-bg-primary) ×4, var(--color-brand-bg), var(--color-button-invert-bg), var(--color-button-invert-bg-hover); literals #ffffff08, #ffffff26, #00000014
evidence.note: Tally of var() references in the bundle as shipped on 2026-07-03.

notes: Marketing-site button, not the product app's; treated as a sample of how Linear's own code consumes its tokens.
